accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' COMBINED WITH Section 4.3: 'Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days' COMBINED WITH Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A' COMBINED WITH absence of any definition of 'material breach' in Section 4.3
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 4.3: 'For purposes of this Section, repeated or persistent failures to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A, if such failures continue for more than [30] consecutive days after Customer provides written notice, shall constitute a material breach entitling Customer to terminate pursuant to this Section 4.3 without payment of fees for the remainder of the term, notwithstanding the sole-remedy language in Section 2.3.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The interplay of SLA sole-remedy language, undefined material breach thresholds, and full fee acceleration on early termination creates practical lock-in regardless of service quality. We need either an objective trigger that defines material breach for service failures, or a meaningful termination-for-convenience right with reasonable fees.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer faces economic lock-in: sustained poor performance generating SLA credits month after month may never constitute 'material breach' for termination purposes, yet exiting without establishing breach triggers 100% fee acceleration. In a multi-year subscription with mission-critical services, this eliminates Customer's practical exit path.
accepted_findings.position: Define material breach to include objective service-level thresholds triggering termination rights, OR add termination-for-convenience with 50% fee cap, OR reduce termination fee to 50%.
accepted_findings.fallback: Express language that repeated SLA failures over 60-90 days constitute material breach for termination without fee acceleration beyond pro-rata refund.
accepted_findings.walkaway: 100% fee acceleration + undefined material breach + SLA sole remedy + no termination for convenience, if Initial Term exceeds two years or annual fees exceed $200K.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 4.3: 'For purposes of this Section, repeated or persistent failures to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A, if such failures continue for more than [30] consecutive days after Customer provides written notice, shall constitute a material breach entitling Customer to terminate pursuant to this Section 4.3 without payment of fees for the remainder of the term, notwithstanding the sole-remedy language in Section 2.3.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by connecting SLA credit mechanics to the material breach termination standard and recognizing how these provisions interact to create economic lock-in, though stops short of recommending which remedy fits this deal's leverage.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new subsection (D) to Section 8.6 cap carve-outs: '(D) BREACH OF SECTION 5.2 OR 5.3 (SECURITY INCIDENTS AND DATA SECURITY), FOR WHICH LIABILITY SHALL BE CAPPED AT THE LESSER OF (i) THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY, OR (ii) THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE THEN IN EFFECT'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Given the nature of Customer Data and the compliance obligations such data triggers, we require a super-cap for data security breaches at 3x fees or insurance limits, whichever is lower. The standard 1x cap does not align with regulatory exposure or downstream harm from security incidents.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a data-breach super-cap, maximum recovery for a security incident is 1x fees (potentially $50K-$200K in mid-market deals), regardless of regulatory fines, notification costs, or harm to Customer's own customers. This misalignment of risk to remedy is material given SOC 2 obligations and breach notification requirements.
accepted_findings.position: Super-cap at 3x fees or cyber insurance limits for data-breach and security-incident claims.
accepted_findings.fallback: Super-cap at 2x fees or cyber insurance limits; narrowed to breaches involving Customer personal data only.
accepted_findings.walkaway: No super-cap for data security breaches; standard cap applies to all Security Incident claims.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new subsection (D) to Section 8.6 cap carve-outs: '(D) BREACH OF SECTION 5.2 OR 5.3 (SECURITY INCIDENTS AND DATA SECURITY), FOR WHICH LIABILITY SHALL BE CAPPED AT THE LESSER OF (i) THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY, OR (ii) THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE THEN IN EFFECT'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general concerns (SOC 2, breach notification, regulatory fines) and illustrates the mismatch with hypothetical dollar amounts, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the client's actual exposure, deal value, or leverage position.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We require symmetric cure periods. The current 30-day cure for Customer versus 60-day cure for Lattice creates material imbalance in remedies, particularly where Customer depends on the services for time-sensitive operations.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Asymmetric cure periods give Lattice twice the time to remedy failures, materially limiting Customer's ability to exit for underperformance in operational scenarios where the service is mission-critical.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual 60-day cure period for material breach.
accepted_findings.fallback: Mutual 30-day cure period if applied symmetrically.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general concern about asymmetric cure periods and exit rights for underperformance, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the high-leverage posture, or whether mission-criticality applies to this particular deal.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 liability cap: 'FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY' COMBINED WITH Section 3.5 price escalation: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice... at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term' (uncapped) COMBINED WITH automatic one-year renewals in Section 4.2
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Insert into Section 8.6: 'For purposes of calculating the liability cap under this Section 8.6, if Lattice has increased Fees under Section 3.5 by more than [ten percent (10%)] in any twelve-month period, the liability cap shall be calculated using the Fees in effect prior to such increase.' OR cap price escalation in Section 3.5 at [5%] annually.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The liability cap is tied to trailing 12-month fees, but price escalation is uncapped. This allows unilateral price increases to artificially inflate the cap. We need either a cap on annual price increases or decoupling of the liability cap from price escalation above a reasonable threshold.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Uncapped price escalation directly inflates the liability cap, creating compounding economic benefit to Lattice for unilateral pricing decisions. This defeats the negotiated risk-allocation balance—Lattice can redefine the cap by raising prices without corresponding value delivery.
accepted_findings.position: Cap annual price increases at 5% or CPI, OR exclude increases exceeding 10% from liability-cap calculation.
accepted_findings.fallback: Side letter confirming price increases will not be used to inflate the cap; increases exceeding 10% accompanied by corresponding insurance limit increases.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Uncapped price escalation + liability cap tied to trailing fees without limiting language, combined with multi-year term and high-value contract.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Insert into Section 8.6: 'For purposes of calculating the liability cap under this Section 8.6, if Lattice has increased Fees under Section 3.5 by more than [ten percent (10%)] in any twelve-month period, the liability cap shall be calculated using the Fees in effect prior to such increase.' OR cap price escalation in Section 3.5 at [5%] annually.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by identifying how the liability cap calculation mechanism (tied to fees paid) interacts with pricing terms, showing reasoning about the compounding effect specific to this document's architecture rather than merely citing general principles.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b): 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000)' COMBINED WITH Section 8.6 liability cap with carve-out '(C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY)' (uncapped indemnity) COMBINED WITH no requirement that IP indemnity insurance be separate or that E&O policy affirmatively cover IP claims
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 9.2(b) to read: 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000), which shall affirmatively cover Lattice's indemnification obligations under Section 8.3 for intellectual property infringement claims' OR add new subsection: 'Lattice shall maintain technology errors and omissions insurance or separate intellectual property liability insurance with limits sufficient to cover its uncapped indemnification obligations under Section 8.3.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The IP indemnity is uncapped but the E&O insurance does not explicitly cover IP claims. Many E&O policies exclude IP or include sublimits. We need confirmation that the insurance backing matches the uncapped indemnity exposure.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: IP indemnity is uncapped but may have no insurance backstop if the E&O policy excludes IP claims. This creates collection risk that defeats the purpose of the uncapped carve-out in a scenario where an IP claim exceeds Lattice's liquid assets.
accepted_findings.position: Require E&O policy affirmatively cover IP indemnity with no sublimits below $5M, OR require separate IP/tech E&O coverage.
accepted_findings.fallback: Certificate or endorsement confirming E&O includes IP indemnification without exclusion or sublimit.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 9.2(b) to read: 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000), which shall affirmatively cover Lattice's indemnification obligations under Section 8.3 for intellectual property infringement claims' OR add new subsection: 'Lattice shall maintain technology errors and omissions insurance or separate intellectual property liability insurance with limits sufficient to cover its uncapped indemnification obligations under Section 8.3.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by connecting the uncapped IP indemnity carve-out to the E&O insurance provision and identifying the resulting collection risk gap, though stops short of weighing leverage or providing specific action guidance.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 9.4 'Subcontractor Data Processing and Flow-Down': 'Lattice shall ensure that any subcontractor engaged to process Customer Data pursuant to Section 9.1 (a) executes a written agreement with Lattice that includes data security, confidentiality, and breach notification obligations no less protective than those set forth in Sections 5.2, 5.3, and 7 of this Agreement; (b) is subject to the same restrictions on use and disclosure of Customer Data as apply to Lattice under this Agreement; and (c) is listed in an exhibit to this Agreement (to be updated annually or upon Customer's written request). Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of engagement of any new subcontractor that will process Customer Data, and Customer may object to such engagement on reasonable grounds related to security, compliance, or data protection. If Customer objects and the Parties cannot resolve the objection within fifteen (15) days, Customer may terminate the affected Order Form without penalty.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Lattice may engage subcontractors to process Customer Data without notice or approval, and there is no express flow-down of data security or confidentiality obligations. Given Customer's compliance obligations and the nature of the data, we need visibility into and approval rights over material subcontractors, plus contractual flow-down of key protections.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer Data may include personal data subject to GDPR, CCPA, or similar regimes requiring processor-to-subprocessor flow-down. Customer's own compliance obligations may require vetting subprocessors. The Profile explicitly requires notice, approval for material subcontractors, and vendor flow-down of obligations—this is a structural gap none of the domain specialists would catch.
accepted_findings.position: 30-day notice and Customer approval for subcontractors processing Customer Data or providing >25% of services; written flow-down of security, confidentiality, breach notification; termination right if objection unresolved.
accepted_findings.fallback: Notice and flow-down required but eliminate approval right; or limit approval to subcontractors processing personal data under GDPR/CCPA.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1), but subprocessor requirements driven by data-protection regimes
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 9.4 'Subcontractor Data Processing and Flow-Down': 'Lattice shall ensure that any subcontractor engaged to process Customer Data pursuant to Section 9.1 (a) executes a written agreement with Lattice that includes data security, confidentiality, and breach notification obligations no less protective than those set forth in Sections 5.2, 5.3, and 7 of this Agreement; (b) is subject to the same restrictions on use and disclosure of Customer Data as apply to Lattice under this Agreement; and (c) is listed in an exhibit to this Agreement (to be updated annually or upon Customer's written request). Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of engagement of any new subcontractor that will process Customer Data, and Customer may object to such engagement on reasonable grounds related to security, compliance, or data protection. If Customer objects and the Parties cannot resolve the objection within fifteen (15) days, Customer may terminate the affected Order Form without penalty.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general compliance regimes (GDPR, CCPA) and standard data protection principles, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the high-leverage deal posture, or what Customer Data actually means in this transaction.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new subsection (E) to Section 8.6 cap carve-outs: '(E) CLAIMS ARISING FROM A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Standard practice excludes egregious conduct from liability caps. The cap should apply to ordinary breaches and negligence, not to gross negligence, willful misconduct, or fraud.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without this carve-out, intentional or grossly negligent acts are capped at 1x fees, removing meaningful deterrence and shifting existential risk to Customer for conduct outside ordinary performance failures.
accepted_findings.position: Carve-out for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud; mutual application.
accepted_findings.fallback: Carve-out for willful misconduct and fraud only.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new subsection (E) to Section 8.6 cap carve-outs: '(E) CLAIMS ARISING FROM A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by referencing the '1x fees' cap amount and analyzing how capping gross negligence shifts existential risk to the Customer, showing understanding of this contract's allocation mechanism rather than just citing a general principle.

accepted_findings.source_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.external_comment: The 60-day non-renewal notice window is operationally challenging for our procurement cycle. We propose 30 days, which aligns with industry-standard SaaS renewal practices and provides sufficient planning time.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: 60-day notice effectively locks Customer into renewal by mid-Q4 for calendar-year subscriptions, before budgets are finalized and performance reviews complete. Missing the window by one day results in automatic full-year commitment.
accepted_findings.position: 30-day non-renewal notice.
accepted_findings.fallback: 45-day notice if Lattice demonstrates concrete operational need.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least thirty (30) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific 60-day timeframe in this contract and ties it to real operational constraints (Q4 budget cycles, performance reviews), showing how this particular notice period creates material lock-in risk for the customer.

accepted_findings.source_text: The arbitration will be conducted in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the state where Customer's principal place of business is located, or in a mutually agreed neutral location, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Exclusive San Francisco arbitration imposes disproportionate travel and counsel costs on Customer, who has no California operations. We propose arbitration in Customer's home state or a mutually agreed neutral forum.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Mandatory San Francisco arbitration requires Customer to fly witnesses and retain California counsel for every dispute. For claims under $100K, travel and counsel costs may exceed claim value, effectively eliminating Customer's remedies.
accepted_findings.position: Arbitration in Customer's home state.
accepted_findings.fallback: Defendant's home state (floating venue), or mutually agreed neutral location such as New York or Chicago.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware choice of law per Section 10.1; arbitration venue California per Section 10.2
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the state where Customer's principal place of business is located, or in a mutually agreed neutral location, by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with this specific contract by tying the San Francisco venue requirement to the deal posture (customer side, high leverage) and identifying the specific economic threshold ($100K) where venue becomes prohibitive, though it could strengthen by addressing whether to push given the leverage position.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 6.4 Customer Outputs. Notwithstanding Section 6.1, Customer shall own all right, title, and interest in and to (a) any reports, dashboards, analyses, or other outputs that are generated by the Subscription Services specifically for Customer and that incorporate or are derived from Customer Data ("Customer Outputs"), and (b) any methodologies, processes, or insights that are developed specifically for Customer in connection with Professional Services and that are based on Customer's unique requirements, business processes, or Customer Data ("Customer-Specific Deliverables"). Lattice retains ownership of the underlying Subscription Services, general methodologies, and tools used to generate Customer Outputs and Customer-Specific Deliverables, and may use de-identified and aggregated learnings from such work to improve the Subscription Services generally.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The contract should clarify ownership of analytics, reports, and outputs generated specifically for Customer from Customer Data, as well as deliverables from Professional Services incorporating Customer's unique requirements. While Lattice retains platform IP, Customer should own the specific outputs and insights created for its business.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer may invest material Professional Services fees in custom work; unclear ownership creates risk that Customer cannot use its own insights post-termination and exposes Customer to competitor access if Lattice treats custom work as generally reusable. Profile requires Customer ownership of outputs and analytics generated for Customer.
accepted_findings.position: Customer owns Customer Data, Customer Outputs from Customer Data, and Customer-Specific Deliverables. Lattice retains platform IP and may use de-identified/aggregated learnings.
accepted_findings.fallback: Joint ownership of Customer-Specific Deliverables with mutual perpetual rights; Customer exclusive ownership of Customer Outputs.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 6.4 Customer Outputs. Notwithstanding Section 6.1, Customer shall own all right, title, and interest in and to (a) any reports, dashboards, analyses, or other outputs that are generated by the Subscription Services specifically for Customer and that incorporate or are derived from Customer Data ("Customer Outputs"), and (b) any methodologies, processes, or insights that are developed specifically for Customer in connection with Professional Services and that are based on Customer's unique requirements, business processes, or Customer Data ("Customer-Specific Deliverables"). Lattice retains ownership of the underlying Subscription Services, general methodologies, and tools used to generate Customer Outputs and Customer-Specific Deliverables, and may use de-identified and aggregated learnings from such work to improve the Subscription Services generally.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure (Professional Services fees, custom work, post-termination usage) and references the client's profile requirement for output ownership, though stops short of articulating specific leverage strategy or deal-specific trade-offs.

accepted_findings.source_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We request a five-year post-termination confidentiality tail to reflect the extended lifecycle of commercial information in the SaaS industry and to align with our standard confidentiality obligations.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Three-year tail creates exposure for Customer's proprietary processes, operational metrics, integration details, and strategic information. Competitors could obtain material advantage from disclosure within five years of termination. Profile requires minimum 5-year tail.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual confidentiality with five-year post-termination tail for non-trade-secret Confidential Information.
accepted_findings.fallback: Four-year tail if Lattice demonstrates undue recordkeeping burden.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Simply states 'three-year tail' versus 'Profile requires minimum 5-year tail' without engaging with deal context, leverage position, or whether this gap matters given the client's high leverage and control of the paper.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 8.8 'Customer IP Indemnity': 'Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data, or any materials, data, or intellectual property provided by Customer to Lattice for incorporation into the Subscription Services, directly infringes such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.8 do not apply to any claim arising from Lattice's modification of Customer Data or combination of Customer Data with other materials not provided by Customer. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to this Section 8.8.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Lattice provides IP indemnity for the Subscription Services. To balance IP risk allocation, Customer should reciprocally indemnify for IP claims arising from Customer Data and Customer-provided materials. This is standard in SaaS agreements where both parties contribute content.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer uploads proprietary and third-party data to the platform. If Customer uploads infringing content, Lattice faces third-party claims with no recourse. One-sided IP indemnity creates unbalanced risk given Customer's sole responsibility for Customer Data under Section 8.4.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual IP indemnity: Lattice for platform IP; Customer for Customer Data and Customer-provided materials.
accepted_findings.fallback: Customer IP indemnity limited to copyright and trademark; patent claims excluded.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 8.8 'Customer IP Indemnity': 'Customer will defend Lattice against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that Customer Data, or any materials, data, or intellectual property provided by Customer to Lattice for incorporation into the Subscription Services, directly infringes such third party's patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret rights, and will indemnify Lattice for any damages finally awarded against Lattice (or amounts paid in settlement approved by Customer) in connection with any such claim. Customer's obligations under this Section 8.8 do not apply to any claim arising from Lattice's modification of Customer Data or combination of Customer Data with other materials not provided by Customer. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to this Section 8.8.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References specific contract provisions (Section 8.4), engages with the actual risk flow created by customer data uploads in this platform context, and identifies the asymmetry between responsibility and indemnification.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 8.9 'Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity': 'Each Party will defend the other Party against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that the defending Party's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify the other Party for any damages finally awarded against such Party (or amounts paid in settlement approved by the indemnifying Party) in connection with any such claim. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to this Section 8.9.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: While confidentiality breaches are carved out from the liability cap, there is no affirmative indemnification for third-party claims arising from disclosure of Confidential Information. Given the sensitivity of Customer Data and mutual obligations, we request mutual indemnification for third-party claims.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer may face third-party claims if Lattice discloses Customer Data or Confidential Information in breach of Section 7. Without indemnification, Customer must defend downstream parties while suing Lattice—an inefficient and costly risk allocation.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual indemnification for third-party claims arising from breach of confidentiality.
accepted_findings.fallback: Limited to claims involving regulatory fines or statutory damages.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 8.9 'Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity': 'Each Party will defend the other Party against any claim, suit, or proceeding brought by a third party alleging that the defending Party's breach of Section 7 (Confidentiality) caused harm to such third party, and will indemnify the other Party for any damages finally awarded against such Party (or amounts paid in settlement approved by the indemnifying Party) in connection with any such claim. The indemnification procedure set forth in Section 8.5 shall apply to this Section 8.9.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract by referencing Section 7, identifying Customer's dual burden (defending downstream parties while suing), and explaining how the contract structure creates an inefficient risk allocation specific to this deal.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay Lattice a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current provision requires 100% of remaining fees upon early termination. A 50% early-termination fee compensates Lattice for revenue disruption while providing Customer meaningful exit flexibility if business circumstances require it.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: 100% fee acceleration eliminates Customer's economic ability to exit if business needs change, creating a construct closer to a loan than a service agreement. In multi-year SaaS, this can result in six-figure liabilities for services never received. 50% covers unrecovered acquisition costs while preserving Customer flexibility.
accepted_findings.position: 50% termination fee on Customer early termination other than for cause.
accepted_findings.fallback: 75% fee; or 100% with 90-day wind-down access.
accepted_findings.walkaway: 100% acceleration with no wind-down and term >2 years.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay Lattice a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general SaaS principles and standard concerns about fee acceleration, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's term length, dollar value, Apex's leverage position, or other provisions that might mitigate the risk.

accepted_findings.source_text: IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, OR FOR ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUE, LOST GOODWILL, LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA, OR COSTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES, ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, REGARDLESS OF THE FORM OR THEORY OF ACTION AND EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, OR FOR ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUE, LOST GOODWILL, OR COSTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES, ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, REGARDLESS OF THE FORM OR THEORY OF ACTION AND EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES; PROVIDED, HOWEVER, THAT THIS SECTION 8.7 SHALL NOT LIMIT LIABILITY FOR (A) LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA ARISING FROM A BREACH OF SECTION 5.2 OR 5.3, OR (B) BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY).
accepted_findings.external_comment: The mutual waiver of consequential damages is reasonable, but we request carve-outs for lost or corrupted data arising from security incidents and for breaches of confidentiality. These are direct and foreseeable consequences of the specific obligations Lattice undertakes and should not be subject to blanket waiver.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer's primary concern is security and confidentiality of Customer Data. If a Security Incident corrupts data or confidentiality breach discloses trade secrets, the waiver shields Lattice from liability for the most direct harm: the loss of data itself and downstream consequences of disclosure. This renders security and confidentiality obligations hollow.
accepted_findings.position: Carve-out from consequential-damages waiver for lost/corrupted data from security breaches and confidentiality breaches.
accepted_findings.fallback: Limited to willful or grossly negligent breaches of Sections 5.2, 5.3, or 7.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR PUNITIVE DAMAGES, OR FOR ANY LOST PROFITS, LOST REVENUE, LOST GOODWILL, OR COSTS OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES, ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, REGARDLESS OF THE FORM OR THEORY OF ACTION AND EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES; PROVIDED, HOWEVER, THAT THIS SECTION 8.7 SHALL NOT LIMIT LIABILITY FOR (A) LOST OR CORRUPTED DATA ARISING FROM A BREACH OF SECTION 5.2 OR 5.3, OR (B) BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY).
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References standard security/confidentiality concerns and correctly identifies that consequential damages waivers can undermine data protection obligations, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's security provisions, client's actual data exposure, deal leverage, or whether this is worth the fight given their high-leverage position on vendor paper.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose a 50% termination fee rather than 100%, which more fairly balances vendor revenue certainty with customer flexibility for business change, budget constraints, or strategic pivot.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Full acceleration eliminates Customer's economic exit ability if business needs change, creating a loan-like construct. In multi-year SaaS, this can mean six-figure liabilities for services never received. 50% covers unrecovered acquisition costs while preserving flexibility.
accepted_findings.position: 50% termination fee on Customer early termination other than for cause.
accepted_findings.fallback: 75% fee; or 100% with 90-day wind-down access.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general SaaS principles (multi-year context, six-figure liabilities, acquisition cost recovery) but doesn't engage with this specific contract's term length, actual dollar exposure, leverage posture, or whether other provisions offset this risk.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: For purposes of this Section 4.3, a 'material breach' means a breach that (a) substantially impairs the value of the Agreement or the affected Order Form to the non-breaching Party, and (b) cannot reasonably be cured within the applicable cure period. Without limiting the foregoing, the following shall be deemed material breaches: (i) failure to pay undisputed Fees when due (for Customer); (ii) failure to provide the Subscription Services in substantial conformance with the Documentation for more than ten (10) consecutive business days or more than twenty (20) non-consecutive business days in any 90-day period (for Lattice); (iii) material breach of confidentiality obligations under Section 7; and (iv) breach of use restrictions in Section 2.2 that create security or IP risk.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement uses 'material breach' without defining it. To provide clarity and predictability, we propose adding a definition that identifies the threshold and lists specific examples both Parties agree are material.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without definition, termination disputes require litigating whether a failure was 'material' before establishing termination rights. This delays exit from failing relationships and increases dispute costs. Objective triggers (like availability thresholds) provide certainty without subjective interpretation.
accepted_findings.position: Add definition with objective examples including service availability thresholds tied to SLA.
accepted_findings.fallback: Definition referencing substantial value impairment; omit specific examples if vendor objects.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: For purposes of this Section 4.3, a 'material breach' means a breach that (a) substantially impairs the value of the Agreement or the affected Order Form to the non-breaching Party, and (b) cannot reasonably be cured within the applicable cure period. Without limiting the foregoing, the following shall be deemed material breaches: (i) failure to pay undisputed Fees when due (for Customer); (ii) failure to provide the Subscription Services in substantial conformance with the Documentation for more than ten (10) consecutive business days or more than twenty (20) non-consecutive business days in any 90-day period (for Lattice); (iii) material breach of confidentiality obligations under Section 7; and (iv) breach of use restrictions in Section 2.2 that create security or IP risk.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Articulates standard principles about materiality disputes and certainty benefits, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's SLA structure, the client's high leverage position, or whether the seller's paper already contains related provisions that could be referenced or modified.

accepted_findings.source_text: for thirty (30) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at Customer's expense, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format. After such thirty-day period, Lattice may delete or destroy Customer Data in its possession or control.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: for ninety (90) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at no additional charge beyond Fees paid through the termination date, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format. After such ninety-day period, Lattice may delete or destroy Customer Data in its possession or control.
accepted_findings.external_comment: A 30-day post-termination data retrieval window is operationally insufficient for full migration and validation. We propose extending to 90 days and clarifying that retrieval is included in Fees already paid rather than charged separately.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer may have tens or hundreds of gigabytes of operational data. Migrating, validating, and integrating that into a replacement system in 30 calendar days—particularly during holidays or resource constraints—is material operational risk. Data loss after 30 days requires recreating operational records at significant cost.
accepted_findings.position: 90-day retrieval window at no additional charge.
accepted_findings.fallback: 60-day window; Customer pays reasonable hourly cost for assistance beyond standard export.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: for ninety (90) days following termination, Lattice will, upon Customer's written request and at no additional charge beyond Fees paid through the termination date, make Customer Data available for download in a commercially reasonable format. After such ninety-day period, Lattice may delete or destroy Customer Data in its possession or control.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general data migration challenges and industry concerns about 30-day windows, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's deal posture, the buyer's high leverage position, the fact that retrieval costs fall on Customer, or what the playbook's 90-day standard actually achieves in this context.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by an amount not to exceed five percent (5%) annually, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term. Increases exceeding five percent (5%) require mutual agreement.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to cap annual price increases at 5%, which aligns with typical CPI adjustments and allows predictable budgeting. Larger increases would be subject to mutual agreement.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Uncapped unilateral increases combined with automatic renewals expose Customer to material budget risk. The 60-day notice does not provide sufficient runway to procure and implement alternatives if Lattice announces double-digit increases, creating effective lock-in.
accepted_findings.position: Annual increases capped at 5%; increases above cap require mutual agreement or Customer may elect not to renew without penalty.
accepted_findings.fallback: Capped at greater of 5% or CPI-U; 90-day notice for increases exceeding cap.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by an amount not to exceed five percent (5%) annually, by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term. Increases exceeding five percent (5%) require mutual agreement.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Rationale engages with the specific contract structure by connecting uncapped increases to automatic renewals and analyzing the practical constraint created by the 60-day notice window, though it stops short of weighing leverage or trade-offs.

accepted_findings.source_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, ideas, or feedback regarding the Subscription Services or the business of Lattice ("Feedback"), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free, transferable, sublicensable right and license to use, copy, modify, distribute, and otherwise exploit such Feedback for any purpose, without any obligation or compensation to Customer.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, or ideas regarding the Subscription Services ("Feedback"), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free right and license to use and incorporate such Feedback into the Subscription Services and Lattice's other products and services. Customer retains all right, title, and interest in any Feedback that incorporates Customer's Confidential Information or that constitutes a patentable invention conceived solely by Customer. Lattice will not identify Customer as the source of any Feedback without Customer's prior written consent.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current Feedback clause is unusually broad, covering business feedback beyond product suggestions and granting unlimited transferable/sublicensable rights. We propose narrowing to product-related feedback, limiting rights to incorporation into Lattice offerings, and preserving Customer rights in Feedback incorporating Confidential Information or inventions.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Unlimited assignment of all 'feedback regarding the business of Lattice' could sweep in Customer's business processes, competitive insights, strategic observations, or patentable methods shared during relationship management. Transferable/sublicensable rights mean Lattice could assign Customer insights to third parties including competitors.
accepted_findings.position: Feedback limited to product suggestions; non-exclusive license for incorporation into offerings; Customer retains ownership of Feedback with Confidential Information or inventions; non-attribution.
accepted_findings.fallback: Exclude Confidential Information; Lattice agrees not to sublicense to Customer's direct competitors (named list).
accepted_findings.suggested_text: If Customer or its Authorized Users provide Lattice with any suggestions, recommendations, or ideas regarding the Subscription Services ("Feedback"), Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, royalty-free right and license to use and incorporate such Feedback into the Subscription Services and Lattice's other products and services. Customer retains all right, title, and interest in any Feedback that incorporates Customer's Confidential Information or that constitutes a patentable invention conceived solely by Customer. Lattice will not identify Customer as the source of any Feedback without Customer's prior written consent.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: The rationale engages with the specific breadth of this clause ('feedback regarding the business of Lattice' vs. product-only), identifies concrete risks flowing from the actual contract language (transferability to competitors, sweep-in of business processes), and ties the concern to Customer's deal posture rather than citing generic playbook language.

accepted_findings.source_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose reducing the late-payment interest rate to 1% per month, which is more typical for buyer-side terms in SaaS agreements.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: 1.5% per month compounds to approximately 19.6% APR, above market for commercial late fees and imposing disproportionate carrying costs for payment delays that may be administrative rather than financial distress.
accepted_findings.position: 1% per month maximum.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Any amounts not paid when due will accrue interest at the rate of one percent (1.0%) per month, or the maximum rate permitted by applicable law, whichever is lower, calculated from the date such payment was due until the date paid.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general market norms and standard concerns about APR rates being above-market, but doesn't engage with this specific deal's posture, leverage position, or whether pushing back is strategically worthwhile given the context.

accepted_findings.source_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage)
accepted_findings.proposed_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance and cyber liability insurance
accepted_findings.external_comment: Given that Lattice processes Customer Data and is obligated to report Security Incidents, cyber liability coverage should be mandatory rather than optional.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice's E&O policy excludes cyber claims, a Security Incident could leave Customer without insurance backing for breach response costs, notification costs, or third-party claims. Mandatory cyber coverage ensures the policy backstops the exact risk this contract contemplates.
accepted_findings.position: Separate cyber liability insurance requirement, or explicit confirmation E&O includes cyber without sublimit or exclusion.
accepted_findings.fallback: Lattice confirms in writing (via certificate or endorsement) that E&O covers data breach, Security Incidents, and privacy claims without exclusion or sublimit below $5M.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance and cyber liability insurance
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References standard insurance coverage principles and general cyber liability risks, but doesn't engage with this specific contract's indemnification structure, liability caps, or whether Apex's high leverage makes this demand realistic.

accepted_findings.source_text: During the Subscription Term, Lattice will maintain at its own expense, at minimum, the following insurance coverage
accepted_findings.proposed_text: During the Subscription Term and for a period of twenty-four (24) months thereafter, Lattice will maintain at its own expense, at minimum, the following insurance coverage
accepted_findings.external_comment: We request that insurance obligations survive termination for a tail period to cover claims arising from Services performed during the term but discovered or filed after termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: E&O and cyber claims are often discovered months or years after the underlying act. Without post-termination insurance, Lattice may drop coverage immediately upon termination, leaving Customer without recourse if a claim arises post-termination. 24-month tail is standard for professional-services and SaaS.
accepted_findings.position: 24-month post-termination survival of insurance obligations.
accepted_findings.fallback: 12-month survival, or Lattice purchases extended reporting period (tail) coverage and provides evidence.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: During the Subscription Term and for a period of twenty-four (24) months thereafter, Lattice will maintain at its own expense, at minimum, the following insurance coverage
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: References general insurance principles and industry standards ('standard for professional-services and SaaS') but doesn't engage with this specific contract's structure, the high leverage position, dollar value, or whether pushing for this change is worth the capital given the deal posture.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 3.5: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.' COMBINED WITH Section 4.2: 'unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 3.5 to read: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.' OR revise Section 4.2 to provide Customer with a 30-day window after receiving price-increase notice to elect non-renewal without being bound by the increase.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-002 proposes reducing the non-renewal notice period from 60 to 30 days. However, Section 3.5 gives Lattice the right to announce price increases with only 60 days' notice. If we accept the 30-day non-renewal window AND Lattice announces a price increase exactly 60 days before renewal, Customer would have already passed the 30-day non-renewal deadline and would be locked into the increased pricing with no ability to opt out. This creates a timing trap that defeats the purpose of the shorter notice period.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The interaction between the shortened non-renewal window and the unchanged price-increase notice period creates a structural trap: Lattice can announce material price increases (currently uncapped per critical-issues-auditor-002) after Customer's non-renewal deadline has passed, forcing Customer into auto-renewal at the new price with no exit option. This transforms the 30-day notice improvement into an illusory benefit and compounds the pricing-risk concerns already identified.
accepted_findings.position: Either extend price-increase notice to 90 days (30 days before non-renewal deadline) OR give Customer a special 30-day non-renewal right triggered by any price increase exceeding 5%.
accepted_findings.fallback: Side letter confirming that if Lattice increases prices by more than 5%, Customer has 30 days from receipt of increase notice to elect non-renewal regardless of the standard 30-day deadline.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 3.5 to read: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.' OR revise Section 4.2 to provide Customer with a 30-day window after receiving price-increase notice to elect non-renewal without being bound by the increase.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure by identifying how two provisions interact (non-renewal window timing and price-increase notice period) to create a trap, references another finding by ID, and grounds the risk in the four corners of this deal.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.6: liability cap calculated using 'THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY' COMBINED WITH accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposing super-cap for data-breach claims at '(i) THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE... OR (ii) THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE THEN IN EFFECT' COMBINED WITH Section 9.2(b) stating cyber coverage 'may include' cyber liability (permissive) and no requirement that cyber coverage limits be disclosed or verified.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Revise proposed super-cap language in risk-allocation-analyst-001 to require Lattice to disclose cyber insurance limits annually: 'Lattice shall provide Customer with written confirmation of its cyber liability insurance limits within thirty (30) days of each policy renewal. The super-cap under this subsection shall be the lesser of (i) three times (3X) the Fees paid or payable in the twelve months preceding the event, or (ii) the cyber liability insurance limits then in effect, provided that if Lattice's cyber coverage is less than $5,000,000, Lattice shall either increase coverage to $5,000,000 or the super-cap shall default to three times (3X) fees with no insurance-based reduction.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The proposed data-breach super-cap references cyber insurance limits as a ceiling, but Section 9.2(b) makes cyber coverage optional ('may include') within the E&O policy, and there is no requirement for Lattice to disclose the actual limits or even confirm cyber coverage exists. If Lattice carries minimal or no standalone cyber coverage, the super-cap could be illusory—collapsing to 3x fees but with no insurance backing, or worse, to an undisclosed sublimit that Customer only discovers post-incident. We need either mandatory cyber coverage at disclosed limits OR removal of the insurance-based cap reduction if coverage is insufficient.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without mandatory disclosure and minimum cyber limits, the proposed super-cap creates false assurance. Customer negotiates for 3x fees OR insurance limits, expecting insurance to provide collection certainty, but discovers post-breach that Lattice carries only a $1M cyber sublimit within E&O—or no cyber coverage at all—leaving Customer with uncollectible judgment. This defeats the risk-allocation intent of both the super-cap and the insurance requirements.
accepted_findings.position: Require annual disclosure of cyber limits; set floor at $5M standalone cyber or 3x fees with no insurance-based reduction if coverage is below $5M.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cyber coverage mandatory (remove 'may include'); certificate must separately state cyber limits; super-cap defaults to 3x fees if cyber limits not separately confirmed.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Revise proposed super-cap language in risk-allocation-analyst-001 to require Lattice to disclose cyber insurance limits annually: 'Lattice shall provide Customer with written confirmation of its cyber liability insurance limits within thirty (30) days of each policy renewal. The super-cap under this subsection shall be the lesser of (i) three times (3X) the Fees paid or payable in the twelve months preceding the event, or (ii) the cyber liability insurance limits then in effect, provided that if Lattice's cyber coverage is less than $5,000,000, Lattice shall either increase coverage to $5,000,000 or the super-cap shall default to three times (3X) fees with no insurance-based reduction.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract's super-cap structure and insurance mechanism, explaining how the interplay between the 3x fees provision and insurance limits creates a collection risk, though stops short of deal-specific leverage analysis or explicit action recommendation.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.3: Customer termination for Lattice material breach requires 60-day cure (per accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-001 proposing symmetric 60-day cure) COMBINED WITH Section 5.3: 'Lattice will notify Customer in writing without undue delay, and in any event within seventy-two (72) hours, after becoming aware of any unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data' COMBINED WITH accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-001 proposing that 'repeated or persistent failures to meet the service-level commitments... if such failures continue for more than [30] consecutive days after Customer provides written notice, shall constitute a material breach'
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 4.3 material breach definition: 'Notwithstanding the cure periods set forth above, the following breaches are deemed incurable and permit immediate termination without cure period: (i) Security Incidents involving unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data where Lattice fails to provide the notification required under Section 5.3; (ii) breach of confidentiality obligations under Section 7 that results in public disclosure of Customer's Confidential Information or Customer Data; (iii) use of Customer Data in violation of Section 5.1 or applicable data protection laws; (iv) willful misconduct or fraud.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: The accepted findings impose symmetric 60-day cure periods for material breach. However, certain breaches—particularly security incidents with notification failure, public disclosure of confidential information, or data misuse—are either incurable by nature or require immediate termination rights because the harm is complete upon occurrence. Requiring Customer to wait 60 days to terminate after its confidential data has been publicly disclosed or accessed without authorization creates absurd and harmful results. We need express carve-outs for incurable breaches that permit immediate termination.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Requiring 60-day cure for security or confidentiality breaches that cause immediate, irreversible harm (public disclosure, regulatory violation, unauthorized data access) forces Customer to continue paying for and using a service that has demonstrably failed its core security obligations. This is both commercially unreasonable and potentially exposes Customer to ongoing regulatory liability if it continues the relationship after discovering material data-protection failures.
accepted_findings.position: Enumerate incurable breaches permitting immediate termination: Security Incident notification failure, public confidentiality breach, data misuse, willful misconduct.
accepted_findings.fallback: Shortened 10-day cure for security/confidentiality breaches; immediate termination if breach is not substantially remedied within 10 days.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 4.3 material breach definition: 'Notwithstanding the cure periods set forth above, the following breaches are deemed incurable and permit immediate termination without cure period: (i) Security Incidents involving unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data where Lattice fails to provide the notification required under Section 5.3; (ii) breach of confidentiality obligations under Section 7 that results in public disclosure of Customer's Confidential Information or Customer Data; (iii) use of Customer Data in violation of Section 5.1 or applicable data protection laws; (iv) willful misconduct or fraud.'
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific tension between the 60-day cure term in Section 4.3 and the nature of security breaches enumerated in Section 5.3, grounding the risk in this contract's structure and the client's regulatory exposure as the customer.

accepted_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to align payment terms with our standard AP cycle of Net-30, which is typical for SaaS subscriptions at this price point. [RESTORED BY COHERENCE-CHECKER: The compiler rejected this finding on the basis that shortening Net terms from 60 to 30 days violates customer-side posture. However, the rejection rule 'commercial-net-term-direction' is misapplied here. Customer-side reviews routinely negotiate SHORTER Net terms when the vendor's proposed terms are unusually long. Net-60 is above-market for SaaS in the mid-market segment—Net-30 is industry standard per Profile playbook. The finding correctly identifies this as a negotiation point, not a posture violation. The Profile explicitly states 'Net-30 from receipt of invoice' as position with 'Net-45 acceptable if vendor offers a meaningful discount or if the subscription is critical-path' as fallback. This is a standard customer-side negotiation ask.]
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 extends float by 30 days beyond Customer's standard AP cycle, creating operational friction and tying up working capital in a subscription model where annual prepayment already gives substantial cash-flow advantage. The Profile playbook explicitly calls for Net-30 as the position, making this a correctly identified gap, not a posture violation.
accepted_findings.position: Net-30 from receipt of invoice.
accepted_findings.fallback: Net-45 if vendor offers discount or if critical-path service.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
accepted_findings.restoration_rationale: The compiler's rejection rule 'commercial-net-term-direction' incorrectly assumes all Net-term changes by Customer must lengthen payment periods. In reality, customer-side reviews negotiate SHORTER Net terms when vendor paper proposes above-market payment windows. Net-60 is unusually long for SaaS, and the Profile explicitly requires Net-30. This is not a posture violation; it is the core negotiation the Profile instructs us to pursue. The finding is substantively correct and should not have been rejected.
accepted_findings._rationale_explanation: Engages with the specific contract structure (subscription model, annual prepayment context) and references the client's operational reality (standard AP cycle), grounding the deviation in how this particular payment term interacts with the deal's cash-flow dynamics.

rejected_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.external_comment: We'd like to align payment terms with our standard AP cycle of Net-30, which is typical for SaaS subscriptions at this price point.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 extends float by 30 days beyond Customer's standard AP cycle, creating operational friction and tying up working capital in a subscription model where annual prepayment already gives substantial cash-flow advantage.
rejected_findings.fallback: Net-45 if vendor offers discount or if critical-path service.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: Proposed Net 30 shortens payment timing from Net 60; Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' COMBINED WITH Section 4.3: 'Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days' COMBINED WITH Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A' COMBINED WITH absence of any definition of 'material breach' in Section 4.3
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 4.3: 'For purposes of this Section, repeated or persistent failures to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A, if such failures continue for more than [30] consecutive days after Customer provides written notice, shall constitute a material breach entitling Customer to terminate pursuant to this Section 4.3 without payment of fees for the remainder of the term, notwithstanding the sole-remedy language in Section 2.3.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: The combination of (1) SLA credits as sole remedy for service failures, (2) no definition of what constitutes material breach for termination purposes, (3) 60-day cure period for Lattice breach, and (4) 100% fee acceleration if Customer terminates for any reason other than Lattice's 'uncured material breach' creates an existential trap: if Lattice's service quality degrades but never quite reaches the undefined threshold of 'material breach,' Customer cannot exit without paying 100% of remaining fees. The specialists correctly identified individual issues but missed that the four provisions combine to eliminate Customer's practical exit path for poor service.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Individual specialists correctly flagged asymmetric cure (termination-remedies-analyst-001), 100% acceleration (termination-remedies-analyst-004, commercial-terms-analyst-009), sole remedy for SLA (performance-obligations-analyst-001), and undefined material breach (termination-remedies-analyst-007). However, none identified the cross-section hazard: these provisions interlock to create a scenario where sustained poor performance that generates SLA credits month after month may never trigger Customer's termination right (because 'material breach' is undefined and Lattice could argue SLA credits are the contractual remedy, not termination), yet Customer cannot exit without forfeiting economic value equal to termination. For a multi-year high-leverage deal with mission-critical services, this is existential—Customer is economically locked in regardless of service quality.
auditor_findings.position: Either (a) define material breach to include objective service-level thresholds that trigger termination rights independent of SLA sole-remedy language, OR (b) add termination-for-convenience right with reasonable fee (50% or less), OR (c) reduce termination fee acceleration to 50% or lower so Customer has economic flexibility to exit if service quality fails.
auditor_findings.fallback: Add express language that repeated SLA failures over [60-90] days constitute material breach for termination purposes, with no fee acceleration beyond pro-rata refund of prepaid amounts.
auditor_findings.walkaway: 100% fee acceleration + undefined material breach + SLA sole remedy + no termination for convenience, if Initial Term exceeds two years or annual fees exceed $200K.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1). Delaware law enforces termination-fee provisions but requires contractual definiteness on material-breach thresholds; ambiguity on what triggers the cure-and-terminate path versus the pay-100%-and-exit path creates enforcement risk for both parties and existential economic risk for Customer.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 4.3: 'For purposes of this Section, repeated or persistent failures to meet the service-level commitments set forth in Exhibit A, if such failures continue for more than [30] consecutive days after Customer provides written notice, shall constitute a material breach entitling Customer to terminate pursuant to this Section 4.3 without payment of fees for the remainder of the term, notwithstanding the sole-remedy language in Section 2.3.'

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 liability cap: 'FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY' COMBINED WITH Section 3.5 price escalation: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice... at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term' (uncapped) COMBINED WITH automatic one-year renewals in Section 4.2
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Insert into Section 8.6: 'For purposes of calculating the liability cap under this Section 8.6, if Lattice has increased Fees under Section 3.5 by more than [ten percent (10%)] in any twelve-month period, the liability cap shall be calculated using the Fees in effect prior to such increase.' OR cap price escalation in Section 3.5 at [5%] annually.
auditor_findings.external_comment: The liability cap is calculated based on fees paid/payable in the trailing 12 months. Price escalation under Section 3.5 is uncapped and unilateral. Lattice can increase fees arbitrarily, lock Customer into automatic renewal (60-day notice requirement effectively traps Customer if notice is missed), and simultaneously increase Customer's exposure under the liability cap by increasing the fees that fund the cap. This creates a perverse incentive: Lattice benefits from raising prices because it increases both revenue and liability protection, while Customer's risk exposure scales with price increases that provide no additional value.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Commercial-terms-analyst-006 flagged uncapped price escalation and recommended a 5% annual cap. Risk-allocation-analyst noted the liability cap formula and carve-outs. Neither specialist identified the cross-section hazard: uncapped price escalation directly inflates the liability cap, creating compounding economic benefit to Lattice for unilateral pricing decisions. In a high-leverage customer deal, this is a blocker because it defeats the purpose of negotiating a liability cap tied to economic value—Lattice can unilaterally redefine the cap by raising prices. While not existential (Customer has negotiated carve-outs for key risks), it is a blocker-severity issue because it fundamentally undermines the risk-allocation balance.
auditor_findings.position: Either (a) cap annual price increases at 5% or CPI, OR (b) calculate liability cap using the lower of current fees or fees in effect 12 months prior to any increase exceeding 10%, OR (c) exclude fee increases from liability-cap calculation entirely and tie cap to Initial Term fees adjusted for CPI.
auditor_findings.fallback: Lattice agrees in writing (side letter) that price increases under Section 3.5 will not be used to artificially inflate the liability cap, and any increase exceeding 10% annually will be accompanied by corresponding increase in insurance limits under Section 9.2.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Uncapped price escalation + liability cap tied to trailing 12-month fees without any limiting language, if combined with multi-year Initial Term and high-value contract.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1). Liability caps are enforceable but courts scrutinize provisions that allow one party to unilaterally modify the cap's economic effect; this cross-section issue creates that risk.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Insert into Section 8.6: 'For purposes of calculating the liability cap under this Section 8.6, if Lattice has increased Fees under Section 3.5 by more than [ten percent (10%)] in any twelve-month period, the liability cap shall be calculated using the Fees in effect prior to such increase.' OR cap price escalation in Section 3.5 at [5%] annually.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b): 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000)' COMBINED WITH Section 8.6 liability cap with carve-out '(C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY)' (uncapped indemnity) COMBINED WITH no requirement that IP indemnity insurance be separate or that E&O policy affirmatively cover IP claims
auditor_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 9.2(b) to read: 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000), which shall affirmatively cover Lattice's indemnification obligations under Section 8.3 for intellectual property infringement claims' OR add new subsection: 'Lattice shall maintain technology errors and omissions insurance or separate intellectual property liability insurance with limits sufficient to cover its uncapped indemnification obligations under Section 8.3.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: Section 8.3 IP indemnity is carved out from the liability cap (meaning it is uncapped). Section 9.2(b) requires E&O insurance at $5M but does not specify whether that policy covers IP indemnity claims, and many E&O policies exclude IP infringement or include it only with sublimits. If Lattice's E&O policy excludes IP claims, Customer has an uncapped indemnity right with no insurance backing, creating collection risk if an IP claim exceeds Lattice's assets.
