auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Risk-allocation-analyst-001 requested a data-breach super-cap and noted the IP indemnity carve-out is present. Insurance-coverage-analyst-002 flagged that cyber coverage is optional ('may include') rather than mandatory. Neither identified the cross-section hazard: IP indemnity is uncapped (carved out from Section 8.6), but the E&O insurance required in Section 9.2(b) does not explicitly cover IP indemnity and may exclude it. This creates a gap where Customer's most significant risk allocation (uncapped IP indemnity) may have no insurance backstop. Not existential because IP claims against SaaS platforms are relatively rare, but major severity because the absence of insurance backing for uncapped indemnity defeats the purpose of the uncapped carve-out.
auditor_findings.position: Require that E&O policy affirmatively cover IP indemnity claims under Section 8.3 with no sublimits below the $5M policy limit, OR require separate IP/technology E&O coverage.
auditor_findings.fallback: Lattice provides certificate of insurance or policy endorsement confirming that the E&O policy includes coverage for IP indemnification obligations without exclusion or sublimit, and agrees to maintain such coverage throughout the term.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1). Insurance-coverage gaps for uncapped indemnities create collection risk that is independent of jurisdiction but relevant to enforceability of the indemnity in practical terms.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 9.2(b) to read: 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000), which shall affirmatively cover Lattice's indemnification obligations under Section 8.3 for intellectual property infringement claims' OR add new subsection: 'Lattice shall maintain technology errors and omissions insurance or separate intellectual property liability insurance with limits sufficient to cover its uncapped indemnification obligations under Section 8.3.'

auditor_findings.proposed_text: Add new Section 9.4 'Subcontractor Data Processing and Flow-Down': 'Lattice shall ensure that any subcontractor engaged to process Customer Data pursuant to Section 9.1 (a) executes a written agreement with Lattice that includes data security, confidentiality, and breach notification obligations no less protective than those set forth in Sections 5.2, 5.3, and 7 of this Agreement; (b) is subject to the same restrictions on use and disclosure of Customer Data as apply to Lattice under this Agreement; and (c) is listed in an exhibit to this Agreement (to be updated annually or upon Customer's written request). Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of engagement of any new subcontractor that will process Customer Data, and Customer may object to such engagement on reasonable grounds related to security, compliance, or data protection. If Customer objects and the Parties cannot resolve the objection within fifteen (15) days, Customer may terminate the affected Order Form without penalty.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: Section 9.1 allows Lattice to engage subcontractors to perform 'any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data,' with no advance notice, no Customer approval right, and no express flow-down of data security or confidentiality obligations. The Profile requires 'Notice required + customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing customer data or providing >25% of services)' and 'Vendor flowdown of obligations is required.' This is a material omission: subcontractor processing of Customer Data is explicitly permitted, but the Agreement provides no contractual mechanism for Customer to vet those subcontractors or ensure they are bound by equivalent data-security obligations.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Protective-provisions-analyst covered confidentiality and IP ownership. Risk-allocation-analyst covered indemnity and liability. Performance-obligations-analyst covered SLA and warranties. Termination-remedies-analyst covered exit rights and dispute resolution. Insurance-coverage-analyst covered insurance. NONE of these specialists have subcontractor management in their domain. The omission is material because: (1) Customer Data may include personal data subject to GDPR, CCPA, or other data-protection regimes that impose controller/processor obligations and require processor-to-subprocessor flow-down, (2) Customer's own compliance obligations (BAA, GLBA, etc.) may require Customer to vet and approve subprocessors, and (3) the Profile explicitly requires notice and approval for material subcontractors. The specialists structurally would not catch this because none of them own 'subcontractor management' as a line item—it falls between protective-provisions (which looks at IP/confidentiality terms) and performance-obligations (which looks at service delivery). For a mid-market SaaS deal with compliance obligations, this is major severity.
auditor_findings.position: Require 30-day advance notice and Customer approval (not to be unreasonably withheld) for subcontractors processing Customer Data or providing >25% of services; require written flow-down of data security, confidentiality, and breach-notification obligations; provide Customer with termination right if objection to subcontractor cannot be resolved.
auditor_findings.fallback: Require notice and flow-down of obligations but eliminate Customer approval right; or limit approval right to subcontractors processing personal data subject to GDPR/CCPA.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (per Section 10.1), but subprocessor notice/approval requirements are driven by data-protection regimes (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA) that apply based on the nature and location of the data, not by Delaware law. Customer's compliance obligations under those regimes create the materiality of this omission.
auditor_findings.suggested_text: Add new Section 9.4 'Subcontractor Data Processing and Flow-Down': 'Lattice shall ensure that any subcontractor engaged to process Customer Data pursuant to Section 9.1 (a) executes a written agreement with Lattice that includes data security, confidentiality, and breach notification obligations no less protective than those set forth in Sections 5.2, 5.3, and 7 of this Agreement; (b) is subject to the same restrictions on use and disclosure of Customer Data as apply to Lattice under this Agreement; and (c) is listed in an exhibit to this Agreement (to be updated annually or upon Customer's written request). Lattice shall provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of engagement of any new subcontractor that will process Customer Data, and Customer may object to such engagement on reasonable grounds related to security, compliance, or data protection. If Customer objects and the Parties cannot resolve the objection within fifteen (15) days, Customer may terminate the affected Order Form without penalty.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 3.5: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.' COMBINED WITH Section 4.2: 'unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Revise Section 3.5 to read: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.' OR revise Section 4.2 to provide Customer with a 30-day window after receiving price-increase notice to elect non-renewal without being bound by the increase.
coherence_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-002 proposes reducing the non-renewal notice period from 60 to 30 days. However, Section 3.5 gives Lattice the right to announce price increases with only 60 days' notice. If we accept the 30-day non-renewal window AND Lattice announces a price increase exactly 60 days before renewal, Customer would have already passed the 30-day non-renewal deadline and would be locked into the increased pricing with no ability to opt out. This creates a timing trap that defeats the purpose of the shorter notice period.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The interaction between the shortened non-renewal window and the unchanged price-increase notice period creates a structural trap: Lattice can announce material price increases (currently uncapped per critical-issues-auditor-002) after Customer's non-renewal deadline has passed, forcing Customer into auto-renewal at the new price with no exit option. This transforms the 30-day notice improvement into an illusory benefit and compounds the pricing-risk concerns already identified.
coherence_findings.position: Either extend price-increase notice to 90 days (30 days before non-renewal deadline) OR give Customer a special 30-day non-renewal right triggered by any price increase exceeding 5%.
coherence_findings.fallback: Side letter confirming that if Lattice increases prices by more than 5%, Customer has 30 days from receipt of increase notice to elect non-renewal regardless of the standard 30-day deadline.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Revise Section 3.5 to read: 'Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least ninety (90) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.' OR revise Section 4.2 to provide Customer with a 30-day window after receiving price-increase notice to elect non-renewal without being bound by the increase.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 8.6: liability cap calculated using 'THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY' COMBINED WITH accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposing super-cap for data-breach claims at '(i) THREE TIMES (3X) THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE... OR (ii) THE LIMITS OF LATTICE'S CYBER LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE THEN IN EFFECT' COMBINED WITH Section 9.2(b) stating cyber coverage 'may include' cyber liability (permissive) and no requirement that cyber coverage limits be disclosed or verified.
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Revise proposed super-cap language in risk-allocation-analyst-001 to require Lattice to disclose cyber insurance limits annually: 'Lattice shall provide Customer with written confirmation of its cyber liability insurance limits within thirty (30) days of each policy renewal. The super-cap under this subsection shall be the lesser of (i) three times (3X) the Fees paid or payable in the twelve months preceding the event, or (ii) the cyber liability insurance limits then in effect, provided that if Lattice's cyber coverage is less than $5,000,000, Lattice shall either increase coverage to $5,000,000 or the super-cap shall default to three times (3X) fees with no insurance-based reduction.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: The proposed data-breach super-cap references cyber insurance limits as a ceiling, but Section 9.2(b) makes cyber coverage optional ('may include') within the E&O policy, and there is no requirement for Lattice to disclose the actual limits or even confirm cyber coverage exists. If Lattice carries minimal or no standalone cyber coverage, the super-cap could be illusory—collapsing to 3x fees but with no insurance backing, or worse, to an undisclosed sublimit that Customer only discovers post-incident. We need either mandatory cyber coverage at disclosed limits OR removal of the insurance-based cap reduction if coverage is insufficient.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: Without mandatory disclosure and minimum cyber limits, the proposed super-cap creates false assurance. Customer negotiates for 3x fees OR insurance limits, expecting insurance to provide collection certainty, but discovers post-breach that Lattice carries only a $1M cyber sublimit within E&O—or no cyber coverage at all—leaving Customer with uncollectible judgment. This defeats the risk-allocation intent of both the super-cap and the insurance requirements.
coherence_findings.position: Require annual disclosure of cyber limits; set floor at $5M standalone cyber or 3x fees with no insurance-based reduction if coverage is below $5M.
coherence_findings.fallback: Cyber coverage mandatory (remove 'may include'); certificate must separately state cyber limits; super-cap defaults to 3x fees if cyber limits not separately confirmed.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Revise proposed super-cap language in risk-allocation-analyst-001 to require Lattice to disclose cyber insurance limits annually: 'Lattice shall provide Customer with written confirmation of its cyber liability insurance limits within thirty (30) days of each policy renewal. The super-cap under this subsection shall be the lesser of (i) three times (3X) the Fees paid or payable in the twelve months preceding the event, or (ii) the cyber liability insurance limits then in effect, provided that if Lattice's cyber coverage is less than $5,000,000, Lattice shall either increase coverage to $5,000,000 or the super-cap shall default to three times (3X) fees with no insurance-based reduction.'

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 4.3: Customer termination for Lattice material breach requires 60-day cure (per accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-001 proposing symmetric 60-day cure) COMBINED WITH Section 5.3: 'Lattice will notify Customer in writing without undue delay, and in any event within seventy-two (72) hours, after becoming aware of any unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data' COMBINED WITH accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-001 proposing that 'repeated or persistent failures to meet the service-level commitments... if such failures continue for more than [30] consecutive days after Customer provides written notice, shall constitute a material breach'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 4.3 material breach definition: 'Notwithstanding the cure periods set forth above, the following breaches are deemed incurable and permit immediate termination without cure period: (i) Security Incidents involving unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data where Lattice fails to provide the notification required under Section 5.3; (ii) breach of confidentiality obligations under Section 7 that results in public disclosure of Customer's Confidential Information or Customer Data; (iii) use of Customer Data in violation of Section 5.1 or applicable data protection laws; (iv) willful misconduct or fraud.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: The accepted findings impose symmetric 60-day cure periods for material breach. However, certain breaches—particularly security incidents with notification failure, public disclosure of confidential information, or data misuse—are either incurable by nature or require immediate termination rights because the harm is complete upon occurrence. Requiring Customer to wait 60 days to terminate after its confidential data has been publicly disclosed or accessed without authorization creates absurd and harmful results. We need express carve-outs for incurable breaches that permit immediate termination.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: Requiring 60-day cure for security or confidentiality breaches that cause immediate, irreversible harm (public disclosure, regulatory violation, unauthorized data access) forces Customer to continue paying for and using a service that has demonstrably failed its core security obligations. This is both commercially unreasonable and potentially exposes Customer to ongoing regulatory liability if it continues the relationship after discovering material data-protection failures.
coherence_findings.position: Enumerate incurable breaches permitting immediate termination: Security Incident notification failure, public confidentiality breach, data misuse, willful misconduct.
coherence_findings.fallback: Shortened 10-day cure for security/confidentiality breaches; immediate termination if breach is not substantially remedied within 10 days.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 4.3 material breach definition: 'Notwithstanding the cure periods set forth above, the following breaches are deemed incurable and permit immediate termination without cure period: (i) Security Incidents involving unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data where Lattice fails to provide the notification required under Section 5.3; (ii) breach of confidentiality obligations under Section 7 that results in public disclosure of Customer's Confidential Information or Customer Data; (iii) use of Customer Data in violation of Section 5.1 or applicable data protection laws; (iv) willful misconduct or fraud.'

restored_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
restored_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
restored_findings.external_comment: We'd like to align payment terms with our standard AP cycle of Net-30, which is typical for SaaS subscriptions at this price point. [RESTORED BY COHERENCE-CHECKER: The compiler rejected this finding on the basis that shortening Net terms from 60 to 30 days violates customer-side posture. However, the rejection rule 'commercial-net-term-direction' is misapplied here. Customer-side reviews routinely negotiate SHORTER Net terms when the vendor's proposed terms are unusually long. Net-60 is above-market for SaaS in the mid-market segment—Net-30 is industry standard per Profile playbook. The finding correctly identifies this as a negotiation point, not a posture violation. The Profile explicitly states 'Net-30 from receipt of invoice' as position with 'Net-45 acceptable if vendor offers a meaningful discount or if the subscription is critical-path' as fallback. This is a standard customer-side negotiation ask.]
restored_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 extends float by 30 days beyond Customer's standard AP cycle, creating operational friction and tying up working capital in a subscription model where annual prepayment already gives substantial cash-flow advantage. The Profile playbook explicitly calls for Net-30 as the position, making this a correctly identified gap, not a posture violation.
restored_findings.fallback: Net-45 if vendor offers discount or if critical-path service.
restored_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
restored_findings.restoration_rationale: The compiler's rejection rule 'commercial-net-term-direction' incorrectly assumes all Net-term changes by Customer must lengthen payment periods. In reality, customer-side reviews negotiate SHORTER Net terms when vendor paper proposes above-market payment windows. Net-60 is unusually long for SaaS, and the Profile explicitly requires Net-30. This is not a posture violation; it is the core negotiation the Profile instructs us to pursue. The finding is substantively correct and should not have been rejected.

Section 9.2(b) includes 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage)' — appropriate for SaaS platform processing Customer Data per Section 5.1
Section 9.2(b) states E&O 'may include cyber liability coverage' — permissive rather than mandatory for a contract involving Customer Data processing and Security Incidents (Section 5.3)

Section 4.2: 'upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods... unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term.'
Section 4.3: Lattice may terminate if Customer breaches with 30-day cure; Customer may terminate if Lattice breaches with 60-day cure. Asymmetric.

Section 4.3: 'Either Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice if the other Party becomes insolvent, makes an assignment for the benefit of creditors, or files or has filed against it a petition in bankruptcy...'
No change-of-control termination right appears in Section 4 or elsewhere in the contract.
Section 4 provides only for-cause termination and does not grant either party a termination-for-convenience right.
Section 4.4 addresses post-termination payment obligations, data retrieval window, and survival of certain sections.
Section 4.4(c): 30-day data retrieval window at Customer's expense, but no affirmative transition-assistance obligation or pricing for such assistance.

Includes mutual treatment covering Customer Data and Lattice's technical/security information.
